studyMetadata.subtitle1: Who it’s for, what it’s all about, and whether it’s for you
studyMetadata.description1: This study is in four main sections. Each of the first three sections has a passage from the Bible which talks about baptism. Alongside it are some questions about that passage, and some other verses from the Bible to help you understand what’s going on. Section four gives space to plan the more practical details of your baptism.

elements.term1: Acts 2:36–41 · Romans 6:1–14 · Matthew 28:18–20 · Ephesians 4:1–6
elements.question1: These are the four main passages we will work through together across this study. It’s worth bookmarking them now.

elements.text1:
Ruth had been a Christian for three months. She had given her life to Jesus at an evening service, quietly, with tears running down her face, while the congregation sang. Her pastor had prayed with her. Her friend had hugged her for a long time afterwards.

In the weeks that followed, something had genuinely changed. She read her Bible in the early morning before the house woke up. She prayed — not perfectly, but honestly. She had told her sister about Jesus, which had taken more courage than she expected.

But every Sunday, when the church announced an upcoming baptism, she felt something she could not quite name. A pull. A question. A sense that there was something she had not yet done, something that was waiting for her.

She had been baptised as an infant, in a white dress, with her parents and grandparents gathered around. There were photographs. Her mother spoke of it warmly.

Was that enough? she wondered. Or is this something I need to do for myself?

This study is for people like Ruth — people who are thinking seriously about baptism and want to understand what the Bible actually says about it.
What This Study Is For

This is not a study about church tradition, or about what your denomination has always done, or about what your parents expect. It is a study about what the Bible teaches.

By the end of it, you should have a clear understanding of three things:

  • Who baptism is for
  • What baptism is all about — what it means and what it declares
  • Whether it is something you should do, and if so, how to take the next steps

The study is designed to be worked through with a church leader. You may read each chapter on your own and then discuss it together, or you may go through it side by side. Either way, you should not work through it in isolation — baptism is a public act, connected to a local church family, and it is right that a church leader is part of the conversation.

If you are a young person considering baptism, it is important that your parents are involved in thinking it through. Your church leader will know how to help with that.
How to Use This Study

The study has five main chapters, plus this introduction:

  • Chapter 1 — Who is Baptism for?
  • Chapter 2 — What is Baptism all about?
  • Chapter 3 — Baptism and Belonging
  • Chapter 4 — What’s next? (practical preparation)
  • Chapter 5 — My Baptism Notes and Common Questions

Each of the first three chapters contains a Bible passage, some explanation, and questions to think through and discuss. Don’t worry if you find some questions difficult — the point is honest engagement, not correct answers. Some things you will not know yet. That is fine. Your church leader is there to help you.

At the end of each chapter there is a short personal reflection section and a practical challenge for the days ahead.

elements.question1: Before you begin, take a moment to write down what you already think baptism is. Where has that idea come from — your upbringing, your church, something you’ve read or heard?
What is the main question you are hoping this study will answer? What would it mean for you to finish it with clarity?

deeper.question1: Is there anything that makes you hesitant or nervous about the idea of being baptised — a person whose reaction you are worried about, a question you are afraid to ask, or a doubt you have not voiced yet? It is worth naming it honestly at the start.

elements.term1: “I don’t feel ready.”
elements.question1: What if you are not sure whether you are a Christian yet — can you still work through this study?
elements.answer1:
Yes, absolutely. This study will help you think through not only baptism but also what it means to become a Christian in the first place. Chapter 1 deals directly with that question. You do not need to have everything settled before you begin — that is precisely what this study is for.

If, as you work through it, you find that you are not yet sure of your faith in Jesus Christ, your church leader can help you think that through too. Coming to faith and being baptised are closely connected in the New Testament, and your church leader will be glad to walk with you through both.

elements.term1: “I was already baptised as a baby.”
elements.question1: If you were baptised as an infant, does this study still apply to you?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most common questions people bring to a study like this, and it is a good one. Different Christian traditions have different views on infant baptism, and this study will address that question honestly in Chapter 1 and in the bonus questions at the end.

The short answer for now is: yes, this study still applies to you. It will help you understand what baptism means, what the Bible teaches about who it is for, and — with your church leader — how to think through your own situation carefully.

Ruth, in our opening story, faced exactly this question. She is not alone.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement. There are no right or wrong answers — this is simply a starting point for honest reflection.
statements1: I have a clear understanding of what baptism means and why Christians do it.
I feel confident that I have personally put my faith in Jesus Christ.
I feel ready to discuss baptism openly with a church leader.
I understand the difference between being baptised as an infant and being baptised as a believer.
I am approaching this study with a genuine desire to do whatever the Bible teaches, even if it is not what I expected.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Ruth felt a pull she could not quite name — a sense that something was waiting for her. Is there anything in her story that resonates with where you are right now?
As you begin this study, what is your honest prayer? What are you asking God to show you or settle in you by the end of it?

elements.text1:
Baptism is one of the most significant public acts a Christian can take. It is a declaration to the watching world — to your family, your community, and your church — of what Jesus has done for you and in you.

It is not something to be rushed into without understanding. But it is also not something to be put off indefinitely out of uncertainty or fear. Jesus commanded it. His first followers took it seriously. And Christians have been doing it, all over the world and across the centuries, ever since.

This study exists to help you understand it well — so that if and when you are baptised, you know exactly what you are doing, and why.

Let’s begin.

elements.term1: A simple preparation
elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Acts 2:36–41 — the passage at the heart of Chapter 1. Read it slowly, more than once if you can. Notice what it says, what surprises you, and what questions it raises.

Then write down one question you want to bring to the discussion.

elements.question1: Read this passage carefully before you begin — and keep it open as you work through the chapter.

elements.text1:
John had grown up in the church. He had been in Sunday school since he could walk, had memorised more Bible verses than most of the adults around him, and had never — as far as anyone could tell — gone through a phase of rebellion or doubt. He was, by every measure, a good young man from a good Christian family.

When he was seventeen, something shifted. During a week of prayer at his church, he found himself reading Romans 8 alone in his room late at night, and for the first time something moved from his head to somewhere deeper. He was not just reading about Jesus — he was, he realised, trusting him. Depending on him. Not because his parents did, not because it was what his church expected, but because he believed it was true and it was his only hope.

He went to his pastor the following week and said he wanted to be baptised.

His pastor asked him a question that stopped him short: “John — are you a Christian?”

Not “are you from a Christian family?” Not “have you been in church all your life?” But: are you, personally, a Christian?

That is the question this chapter begins with. Because before we can ask who baptism is for, we need to ask what it means to be a Christian — and whether that description fits you.
Part 1 — The First Christian Sermon

To understand who baptism is for, we need to go back to the very beginning of the church — to the day when the first group of people were ever baptised as Christians.

Jesus had died on the cross as a perfect sacrifice, taking the punishment for the sins of his people. He had been buried, and then — because God was fully satisfied with his sacrifice — he had risen from death three days later. He had returned to heaven to reign as King alongside God the Father. And then, just as he had promised, he had sent his Holy Spirit to fill his disciples and enable them to carry his message to the world.

We join the story at a decisive moment. One of Jesus’ disciples, Peter, is preaching the very first Christian sermon to a large crowd in Jerusalem. We pick up at his conclusion.

elements.passageText1:
36“Therefore let all Israel be assured of this: God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah.”

37When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart and said to Peter and the other apostles, “Brothers, what shall we do?”

38Peter replied, “Repent and be baptised, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. 39The promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off — for all whom the Lord our God will call.”

40With many other words he warned them; and he pleaded with them, “Save yourselves from this corrupt generation.” 41Those who accepted his message were baptised, and about three thousand were added to their number that day.
elements.passageText2:
36“Therefore let all the house of Israel know beyond a doubt that God has made this Jesus whom you crucified both Lord and Christ.”

37Now when they heard this, they were acutely distressed and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, “What should we do, brothers?”

38Peter said to them, “Repent, and each one of you be baptised in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. 39For the promise is for you and your children, and for all who are far away, as many as the Lord our God will call to himself.”

40With many other words he testified and exhorted them saying, “Save yourselves from this perverse generation!” 41So those who accepted his message were baptised, and that day about three thousand people were added.

elements.question1: Peter tells the crowd that God has made Jesus — the one they crucified — both Lord and Messiah. The people are described as being “cut to the heart.” What do you think that means? What is it about Peter’s message that produces that reaction?

deeper.question1: Have you ever had a moment when something you heard or read about Jesus landed differently — not just as information, but as something that personally confronted or moved you? What was that like?

elements.question1: The crowd asks: “What shall we do?” Peter gives them two instructions in verse 38. What are they — and what do you think each one means?

elements.term1: Repentance
elements.question1: What does it actually mean to repent? Is it just feeling sorry?
elements.answer1:
Repentance is more than feeling sorry. The word in the original Greek means to change your mind — and that change of mind leads to a change of direction. It means turning away from a life lived on your own terms, and turning toward God and his way.

It is not about achieving a certain level of goodness before you come to God. The people in Acts 2 were baptised on the same day they heard and believed the gospel — they had not yet had time to prove themselves or clean up their lives. What they had done was accept the message, acknowledge their sin, and turn toward Jesus.

Repentance is not a one-time event that you complete perfectly and then move on from. It is the posture of a Christian life — a continual turning back to God when we drift. But it begins with a genuine, personal moment of turning.

elements.question1: Peter says two things are promised to those who repent and are baptised (v38). What are they? What do each of these mean to you personally?
Verse 41 tells us who gets baptised. What is the connection between accepting the message and being baptised — and what does that tell us about the right order of things?

deeper.question1: Is there anything that makes you hesitate to say plainly that you have personally accepted the gospel message — the news that Jesus died for your sins and rose again? If so, what is it?

elements.text1: Part 2 — Am I a Christian?

The passage makes clear that baptism follows believing. So before we go any further, it is worth pausing to ask the most important question in this study — not “should I be baptised?” but “am I a Christian?”

Being a Christian is not the same as growing up in a Christian home, attending church regularly, being a generally good person, or having been baptised or dedicated as a child. All of those things may be true of you. None of them, on their own, make you a Christian.

A Christian is someone who has personally heard the gospel — the news that Jesus Christ died in their place and rose again — and has responded to it with repentance and faith. Not faith inherited from parents. Not faith performed for a congregation. Personal faith. Your own.

elements.question1: In your own words, what is the gospel — the good news about Jesus Christ? Try to explain it as if you were telling someone who had never heard it before.
When did you first hear the gospel? How have you responded to it — not what you have always thought or assumed, but how you have personally, deliberately responded?

deeper.question1: John, in our opening story, described a moment when his faith moved from his head to somewhere deeper — from knowing about Jesus to actually trusting him. Has anything like that happened for you? If you are not sure, what would it mean for that to happen?

elements.term1: “I need to be good enough before I get baptised.”
elements.question1: Should you wait until you have reached a certain level of Christian maturity before being baptised?
elements.answer1:
No. The people baptised in Acts 2 were baptised on the same day they believed — they had not had time to prove themselves, grow in holiness, or demonstrate sustained Christian living. Throughout the New Testament, the pattern is the same: believe, repent, be baptised. There is no waiting period to earn the right.

Baptism is not a reward for mature Christians. It is the first public step of a new Christian. It is for forgiven people, not perfect people — people who have accepted the gospel and turned toward Jesus, and who now want to declare that publicly.

If you are waiting until you feel good enough, you may be waiting a very long time. And you may be misunderstanding what baptism is for.

elements.term1: “I was baptised as a baby — won’t that do?”
elements.question1: Some Christians were baptised as infants. Does that count?
elements.answer1:
This is a genuinely contested question among Christians, and good people disagree about it. Some traditions believe that infant baptism is a valid sign of belonging to a covenant community, anticipating faith that will come later. Other traditions believe that baptism is only meaningful when given to someone who is personally professing faith — and that infant baptism, however well-intentioned, is not what the New Testament has in mind.

The view of this study is that baptism is for people who are able to give a credible, personal profession of faith in Jesus Christ. It is a sign of new spiritual birth — not physical birth into a Christian family.

This does not mean that those who baptise infants are wrong to love and care for their children in that way. But it does mean that if you have come to personal faith in Jesus and have never been baptised as a believer, baptism is something you should prayerfully consider.

Talk this through carefully with your church leader. It is an important conversation, and they will help you think it through well.

elements.text1:
There is one more passage worth reading alongside Acts 2 — a story that shows how seriously the first Christians took the connection between faith and baptism.

In Acts 8:26–40, a man from Ethiopia — a high official in the queen’s court — is reading from the prophet Isaiah when he meets a follower of Jesus named Philip. Philip explains the passage to him, and in doing so tells him the good news about Jesus. As they travel together and pass a body of water, the man asks a simple question: “What is to prevent me from being baptised?”

Nothing, it turns out. He stops the chariot. He goes down into the water. He is baptised. And he goes on his way rejoicing.

He did not wait for a class, or a preparation period, or a slot in the church calendar. The first step of his new life in Jesus was baptism — taken immediately, with joy.

elements.question1: What strikes you about the Ethiopian official’s response to the gospel? What does his urgency tell you about how the first Christians understood baptism?

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I can clearly explain the gospel — the good news about Jesus — in my own words.
I am confident that I have personally repented of my sin and put my faith in Jesus Christ.
I understand that baptism is for people who have personally believed, not just for those from Christian families.
I feel ready to discuss with a church leader whether I am ready to be baptised.
I understand that baptism is not about being good enough — it is about responding to what Jesus has done.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: John’s pastor asked him a simple but searching question: “Are you a Christian?” How would you answer that question — honestly, in your own words, not the answer you think you are supposed to give?
The people in Acts 2 were “cut to the heart” by Peter’s message. Has the gospel ever cut you to the heart in that way? What happened — and what did you do with it?
Based on what you have read in this chapter, do you think baptism is something you should do? What is holding you back — or what is drawing you forward?

elements.text1:
Baptism is not for perfect people. It is not for people who have earned the right through years of faithful service. It is not for people who have been in church the longest, or whose families have the strongest Christian heritage.

It is for people who have heard the gospel, been cut to the heart by it, turned from their sin, and put their trust in the Lord Jesus Christ.

The question is not whether you are good enough. The question is whether you have believed.

elements.term1: Preparation for next time
elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Romans 6:1–14 — the passage at the heart of Chapter 2. As you read it, ask yourself: what does Paul say baptism pictures? What does it say about the old life and the new?

Write down one thing that strikes you — and one question the passage raises for you — to bring to the next discussion.

elements.term1: Romans 6:1–14
elements.question1: Read this passage carefully before you begin — and keep it open as you work through the chapter.

elements.text1:
Sarah had watched three baptisms before her own. She had stood at the edge of the pool with the rest of the congregation, singing, while one by one the candidates went down into the water and came back up again. People clapped. Some wept. The pastor said something each time about death and resurrection, about the old life and the new.

She had found it moving. But she was not sure she had understood it.

When it came to her own baptism preparation, she asked her pastor a question she had been carrying for months: “What is actually happening when someone gets baptised? Is it just a symbol? Does the water do something? Am I a Christian before I go in, or does the water make me one?”

Her pastor smiled. He told her those were exactly the right questions. And then he opened his Bible to Romans 6.
Part 1 — Baptism is a Sign

Before we look at the Romans 6 passage, it is important to establish one foundational truth: baptism does not make you a Christian. It is a sign of what has already happened — a visible, public picture of an invisible, inner reality.

Think of a wedding ring. The ring does not create the marriage. The marriage exists because two people made vows and became husband and wife. The ring is a sign — it points to that reality, declares it publicly, and reminds both the wearer and the world of what is true. Removing the ring does not end the marriage. But wearing it matters. It is a meaningful, deliberate declaration.

Baptism works in a similar way. The water does not wash away your sins — Jesus’ death on the cross does that, and it happens the moment you put your faith in him. But baptism is the moment you declare that publicly, visibly, in the presence of your church family and anyone else who is watching. It is the sign of what Jesus has already done in you.

So what does that sign actually depict? The New Testament gives us three interlocking pictures.
Part 2 — A Bath, a Burial, and a Birth

Baptism as a Bath

The Bible is frank about the human condition: we are spiritually unclean. Our sin has made us unfit to stand before a holy God. Left to ourselves, we have no way to deal with that problem.

The good news of the gospel is that Jesus deals with it for us. Through his death on the cross, our sins are washed away. We are made spiritually clean — not because of anything we have done, but because of what he has done. We can now approach God with confidence, accepted and forgiven.

In Acts 22:16, a man called Ananias says to Paul, just after Paul has come to faith in Jesus: “Get up, be baptised and wash your sins away, calling on his name.” The language of washing is the language of baptism — because baptism is a picture of the spiritual cleansing that has already taken place through faith in Christ.

Going under the water and coming back up declares: I was unclean. Now, through Jesus, I am clean.

elements.question1: In what sense are human beings spiritually “unclean” before God? What has Jesus done about that — and how does the image of a bath help explain it?

deeper.question1: Have you experienced a genuine sense of your own sin — not just as a general acknowledgment, but as a personal weight? How did you respond to it, and what difference has Jesus’ forgiveness made to you?

elements.text1:
Baptism as a Burial and a Birth

The second and third pictures come together in the passage we are about to study. They are inseparable.

When you go down into the water, you are enacting a burial. The old life — the life lived in sin, under the power of sin, for yourself rather than for God — is dead and buried. It goes under the water and does not come back.

When you come up out of the water, you are enacting a birth. You are rising into new life — life that is united with the risen Christ, life that is lived for God, life that death no longer has the final word over.

This is not just poetic language. The apostle Paul, in Romans 6, argues that this death and resurrection is spiritually real — that something has genuinely happened to the person who believes in Jesus. Baptism does not cause it. But baptism pictures it with startling clarity.

elements.passageText1:
1What shall we say, then? Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase? 2By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer? 3Or don’t you know that all of us who were baptised into Christ Jesus were baptised into his death? 4We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life.

5For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we will certainly also be united with him in a resurrection like his. 6For we know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin — 7because anyone who has died has been set free from sin.

8Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him. 9For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, he cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over him. 10The death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God.

11In the same way, count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus. 12Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires. 13Do not offer any part of yourself to sin as an instrument of wickedness, but rather offer yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life; and offer every part of yourself to him as an instrument of righteousness. 14For sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace.
elements.passageText2:
1What then shall we say? Are we to remain in sin so that grace may increase? 2Absolutely not! How can we who died to sin still live in it? 3Or do you not know that as many of us as were baptised into Christ Jesus were baptised into his death? 4Therefore we have been buried with him through baptism into death, in order that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too may live a new life.

5For if we have become united with him in the likeness of his death, we will certainly also be united in the likeness of his resurrection. 6We know that our old man was crucified with him so that the body of sin would no longer dominate us, so that we would no longer be enslaved to sin. 7(For someone who has died has been freed from sin.)

8Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him. 9We know that since Christ has been raised from the dead, he is never going to die again; death no longer has mastery over him. 10For the death he died, he died to sin once for all, but the life he lives, he lives to God.

11So you too consider yourselves dead to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus. 12Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its desires, 13and do not present your members to sin as instruments to be used for unrighteousness, but present yourselves to God as those who are alive from the dead and your members to God as instruments to be used for righteousness. 14For sin will not be your master, because you are not under law but under grace.

elements.question1: Paul says that being baptised into Christ means being baptised into his death. What do you think he means by that? How is a Christian’s experience connected to what happened to Jesus on the cross?

deeper.question1: Paul uses the phrase “united with him” (v5). What does it mean, in your own experience, to be united with Christ — not just to know about him, but to be joined to him in a living relationship? What difference does that union make day to day?

elements.question1: Paul describes the “old self” as crucified with Christ (v6), so that we are “no longer slaves to sin.” What was your old self like — the version of you that lived without Christ at the centre? In what sense is that self now dead?

Verses 7–10 describe Jesus’ own death and resurrection as the pattern for the Christian life. He died to sin once; he now lives to God. What does it mean for you, practically, to “count yourself dead to sin but alive to God” (v11)?

deeper.question1: Paul does not say that sin becomes impossible for a Christian — he says it should no longer be our master (v14). Is there an area of your life where sin still feels more like a master than a defeated enemy? What does this passage say to you about that?

elements.question1: Look at verses 12–13. Paul moves from theology to instruction — from what is true to what we should therefore do. What does he tell us to offer to God, and what does he tell us to withhold from sin? How is baptism a public declaration of exactly that commitment?

elements.term1: “Does the water actually do anything?”
elements.question1: Is baptism just a symbol — or does something real happen when a person is baptised?
elements.answer1:
Baptism is a sign — but that does not mean it is merely a symbol, in the sense of being unimportant or optional. Signs matter. They carry meaning, make declarations, and mark transitions.

What baptism does not do is save you. You are saved by grace through faith in Jesus Christ — full stop. The water cannot add to what his cross has already accomplished.

What baptism does do is several important things: it declares publicly what is already true of you before God; it marks your entry into the life of the local church; it gives you a moment of obedience and commitment that you can look back on; and through the Holy Spirit, it seals upon your heart the reality of what you are declaring. It is a meaningful, significant, commanded act — not a mere formality, but not a second means of salvation either.

Sarah’s question — does the water do something? — is a good one. The answer is: the water declares what Jesus has already done.

elements.term1: “Why go all the way under? Can’t I just be sprinkled?”
elements.question1: Does baptism have to be by full immersion — going completely under the water?
elements.answer1:
The Greek word for “baptise” means to wash, dip, plunge, or submerge — so the weight of the word itself points toward full immersion. And as Romans 6 makes clear, the meaning of baptism is wrapped up in the pictures of burial and resurrection: going down under the water and rising back up. Sprinkling water over someone’s head conveys the picture of washing, but it does not as naturally convey the picture of death, burial, and new birth.

For these reasons, full immersion is the practice commended in this study — it is the method most consistent with the meaning of baptism as the New Testament presents it.

That said, there are situations — such as significant physical disability — where full immersion may not be possible, and a church leader can help think through what is appropriate in those cases.

elements.text1: Part 3 — What Baptism Declares

It is worth pausing to bring together the three pictures and ask: what exactly is a person declaring when they are baptised?

They are declaring, first, that they were spiritually dirty and that Jesus has washed them clean — that their sins are forgiven through his death on the cross.

They are declaring, second, that the old life — the life lived for self, under the power of sin — is over. Dead. Buried. Not coming back.

They are declaring, third, that they have been raised into a new life — a life united with the risen Christ, a life that now belongs to God rather than to sin.

And they are declaring all of this not in a private journal, not in a quiet prayer, but in public — in front of their church, their family, their community. Baptism is an announcement. It says to everyone watching: this is who I am now, this is what Jesus has done for me, and I am not ashamed of it.

elements.question1: Thinking about your own life — your own story of coming to faith — which of the three pictures (bath, burial, birth) feels most significant to you right now, and why?

Baptism is a public declaration. Is there anything about making that declaration publicly — in front of your church, your family, your community — that feels daunting? What might be holding you back from it?

deeper.question1: Romans 6:4 says we are raised “in order that… we too may live a new life.” The point of baptism is not just the moment in the water — it is the life that follows. What does living a new life look like for you, practically, in your home, your relationships, your work?

elements.term1: “Why was Jesus baptised if he had no sin?”
elements.question1: Jesus was baptised by John — but if baptism is about repentance and forgiveness, why did Jesus need it?
elements.answer1:
It is a fair question — and John the Baptist himself raised it (Matthew 3:14). Jesus had not sinned, so he had nothing to repent of. But Jesus told John to go ahead “to fulfil all righteousness.”

There are at least three things going on. First, Jesus was doing what was right — and God the Father confirmed it immediately, declaring from heaven that he was well pleased with his Son. Second, Jesus was giving us a sign of what he was going to do — his own death, burial, and resurrection were the reality to which every believer’s baptism points. Third, Jesus was identifying himself with sinners — standing in the place of the people he came to save, as he would ultimately do on the cross.

Jesus’ baptism was unique. He did not need what baptism declares — forgiveness, new birth, freedom from sin’s power. But by being baptised, he endorsed and inaugurated the practice for all who would follow him.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I understand that baptism is a sign of what Jesus has already done — it does not make me a Christian.
I can explain the three pictures of baptism — bath, burial, and birth — in my own words.
I believe that my old life of sin has genuinely ended and that I am living a new life in Christ.
I understand why full immersion is the method of baptism that best captures what the New Testament teaches.
I feel ready to make the declaration that baptism involves — publicly, in front of my church and community.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Sarah watched three baptisms before her own and found them moving but not fully understood. Has your understanding of what baptism means changed through this chapter? What is clearest to you now that was not before?

Romans 6:4 contains one of the key verses of this study: “We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life.” Write this verse somewhere you will see it this week. What does it mean for the way you live tomorrow?
If you were baptised tomorrow, what would you be declaring to the people watching? Try to put it in your own words — not theological language, but the language of your own story.

elements.text1:
Baptism is not complicated, but it is profound. It takes a few minutes. It involves water, and a few words, and a church family gathered around you. But in those few minutes, you are enacting — for yourself and for everyone watching — the death of the old life and the birth of the new. You are saying, with your whole body, what the gospel has declared about you: I was lost. I am found. I was dead. I am alive. I was dirty. I am clean. And it is all because of Jesus.

That is worth doing. That is worth doing well. And that is worth understanding before you do it.

elements.term1: Preparation for next time
elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1:
Read Matthew 28:18–20 and Ephesians 4:1–6 — the two passages at the heart of Chapter 3. As you read them, ask: what does baptism have to do with belonging? What community does it connect you to, and what does that community look like?

Write down one thing that strikes you from each passage, and bring them to the next discussion.

elements.term1: Matthew 28:18–20 · Ephesians 4:1–6
elements.question1: Read both passages carefully before you begin — and keep them open as you work through the chapter.

elements.text1:
Rachel had not expected it to feel the way it did.

She had prepared her testimony carefully. She had chosen her outfit. She had rehearsed the moment in her mind a dozen times. She had told herself it was about her — her faith, her decision, her declaration.

But when she came up out of the water and opened her eyes, the first thing she saw was the congregation. Her church family. Faces she recognised — the woman who had prayed with her every Tuesday morning, the elder who had answered her questions for three months, the teenagers who had welcomed her when she first walked through the door, the children waving from the front row.

They were not observers. They were part of it.

In that moment she understood something she had not fully grasped before. Baptism was not just between her and God. It was her entering — publicly, formally, joyfully — into a family. A people. A body that had existed long before her and would go on long after.

She belonged now. Not just to God. To them.
Part 1 — Baptism as a Seal

In Chapter 1 we learned that baptism is for people who have personally believed the gospel. In Chapter 2 we learned that baptism is a sign — a visible picture of the spiritual bath, burial, and birth that takes place when a person comes to faith in Jesus.

In this chapter we explore a third dimension of baptism that we have not yet fully addressed. Baptism is not only a sign. It is also a seal.

When you put your faith in Jesus, the Holy Spirit seals upon your heart the truth that you belong to God — that you are his child, fully accepted, fully forgiven, fully his. Baptism is the outward, physical counterpart of that inner sealing. It is the moment you declare, before God and his people: I belong to him. And I belong to them.

That second part — I belong to them — is what this chapter is about.
Part 2 — The Great Commission

Our first passage is one of the most well-known in the New Testament. It records some of Jesus’ final words to his disciples before he returned to heaven — instructions not just for them, but for every generation of his followers since, including you.

elements.passageText1: 18Then Jesus came to them and said, “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. 19Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, 20and teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you. And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age.”
elements.passageText2: 18Then Jesus came up and said to them, “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. 19Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, 20teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you. And remember, I am with you always, to the end of the age.”

elements.question1: Jesus begins by declaring that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to him. Why does he say this before giving the commission? What difference does it make to know that the one sending you out has all authority?

deeper.question1: In what areas of your life do you find it hardest to live as though Jesus has all authority — not just in church, but everywhere? What would it look like to take his authority seriously in those areas?

elements.question1: The central command in verse 19 is “make disciples.” Baptising and teaching are the two activities that accompany that. What do you think a disciple is — and what does it mean to make one? How are baptism and teaching both essential parts of that process?

Jesus says disciples are to be baptised “in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.” Why all three? What does this trinitarian formula tell us about who we are being baptised into relationship with?

deeper.question1: Jesus says disciples are to come from “all nations.” What does it mean that baptism — this one act — is the shared entry point for Christians from every culture, language, and background on earth? How does that change the way you think about your own baptism?

elements.question1: Jesus closes with a promise: “I am with you always, to the very end of the age.” This promise is given specifically to those who are making disciples — living out the commission. What does this promise mean to you personally? Where do you most need to hear it?

elements.text1: Part 3 — One Body, One Baptism

The Great Commission shows us that baptism is connected to the global mission of Jesus — making disciples of all nations. But baptism is also deeply local. It connects you to a specific community of people in a specific place. Our second passage, from Paul’s letter to the church in Ephesus, shows us what that community is meant to look like — and why baptism is part of its shared foundation.

elements.passageText1: 1As a prisoner for the Lord, then, I urge you to live a life worthy of the calling you have received. 2Be completely humble and gentle; be patient, bearing with one another in love. 3Make every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit through the bond of peace. 4There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called to one hope when you were called; 5one Lord, one faith, one baptism; 6one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all.
elements.passageText2: 1I, therefore, the prisoner for the Lord, urge you to live worthily of the calling with which you have been called, 2with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in love, 3making every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. 4There is one body and one Spirit, just as you too were called to the one hope of your calling, 5one Lord, one faith, one baptism, 6one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all.

elements.text1:
A bit of background: Paul is writing to a church in Ephesus made up of people from very different backgrounds — Jewish and Gentile, rich and poor, educated and not. What they had in common was not their culture or their history. It was Christ. Paul’s great argument in this letter is that in Christ, people who had nothing in common have been made into one body.

In verses 4–6 he lists the foundations of that unity. Notice that baptism is in the list — not as a minor detail, but as one of the seven great unities that hold the church together.

elements.header1: “Live a life worthy of the calling” (v1)
elements.question1: Paul urges the Ephesians to live worthy of their calling — and describes that as being humble, gentle, patient, and bearing with one another in love. What does this tell us about what the Christian community is meant to look like? How does this compare with communities you have been part of?

deeper.question1: Paul says to “make every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit through the bond of peace” (v3). Unity is something to be kept, not created — it already exists because of the Spirit. Where in your church community do you see that unity being lived out well? Where is it being threatened?

elements.question1: Paul lists seven “ones” in verses 4–6: one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father. Why do you think baptism is listed here alongside these great theological foundations of Christian unity? What is Paul saying about its significance?

elements.term1: Baptism and Belonging
elements.question1: How does baptism connect you to a community — and not just any community?
elements.answer1:
In the Old Testament, if a foreigner wanted to join God’s people, they needed to be baptised. It was a public act of crossing over — leaving one people and joining another. The language and imagery carried into the New Testament.

When you are baptised, you are not just declaring something about your inner spiritual state. You are making a public crossing. You are saying: I have left the world — a life lived for self, apart from God — and I have joined God’s people. Not just God’s global, heavenly family, but his local, embodied, gathered family: the church.

This is why baptism matters so much to the community that witnesses it. When Rachel came up out of the water and saw her church family, they were not passive spectators. They were receiving her. She was, in that moment, formally and publicly joining them. And they were welcoming her in.

elements.text1: Part 4 — Belonging Locally

Baptism connects you to the global church — every believer, in every nation, in every century, who has been baptised in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. That is a staggering thought. You are joining a family that stretches across the whole of human history.

But that global belonging is always experienced locally. You cannot be part of the abstract global church without being part of a real, local, imperfect, gathered community of believers in a specific place.

Being part of a local church is not optional for a Christian. It is not an extra, for those who enjoy that sort of thing. It is where you are taught the Bible, where you are held accountable, where you serve and are served, where you are encouraged when you struggle and challenged when you drift. It is the community that will celebrate your baptism — and then walk with you in everything that follows.

elements.question1: Are you part of a local church at the moment? What does your involvement look like — and is it the kind of belonging that Paul describes in Ephesians 4?

deeper.question1: What would it mean for you to be genuinely committed to a local church — not just attending, but belonging, serving, and being known? What would you need to change, give up, or step into for that to be true?

elements.question1: Rachel’s baptism surprised her — it turned out to be as much about the community as about her own declaration. Is that how you have thought about baptism until now? How does this chapter change or deepen your understanding of what baptism is for?

elements.term1: “Do I have to be baptised in front of the church?”
elements.question1: Is it necessary for baptism to happen publicly, in front of a congregation?
elements.answer1:
The Bible does not command a specific location or audience for baptism. But baptism is, by its very nature, a public act. It is a declaration — made before witnesses — of what Jesus has done and of the community you are joining. Doing it privately, away from the church, would miss much of its meaning.

The local church wants to see it. For them, witnessing a baptism is an encouragement — a reminder of the gospel, a celebration of new life, a moment of shared joy. For you, having your church family present makes it an act of genuine belonging, not just a private religious experience.

If there are particular reasons why a public baptism feels complicated — family pressure, safety concerns, or other circumstances — your church leader will help you think through the wisest approach. But the default should always be: in front of the church, together, as a family.

elements.term1: “Do I need to be baptised before I can take communion?”
elements.question1: What is the relationship between baptism and communion — and does the order matter?
elements.answer1:
Communion and baptism are closely related. Both are acts commanded by Jesus. Both are signs of something spiritually real. Both are connected to belonging to God’s family.

The normal pattern in the New Testament and in church life is that a person is baptised first, and then begins taking communion as part of the church family. If you think of baptism as marking your entry into God’s family, communion marks your continuing in it — taken regularly, together, as a community remembrance of Jesus’ death.

If you have been taking communion before you have been baptised, you have not committed a serious sin. But this study would encourage you to get baptised — and your church leader can help you take that step.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I understand that baptism is not just about my relationship with God — it is also about joining his people.
I am actively part of a local church community where I am known and where I belong.
I understand why the Great Commission connects making disciples with baptising and teaching.
I see baptism as a public declaration, not just a private spiritual experience.
I feel ready to be welcomed into my church family through baptism.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Rachel came up out of the water and saw her church family — and understood for the first time that baptism was about belonging, not just declaring. Is there a community of people whose faces you would want to see when you come up out of the water? Who are they?

Paul says there is “one baptism.” Every Christian who has ever been baptised has enacted the same declaration — across every culture, century, and continent. What does it mean to you to join that vast, global, historical family through this one act?
Jesus promises: “I am with you always, to the very end of the age.” As you approach baptism — and the life of discipleship that follows — where do you most need to hold on to that promise?

elements.text1:
You have now worked through the three core questions of this study: who baptism is for, what it means, and what community it connects you to.

The next step is a decision. Not a complicated one — but a real one.

Are you going to be baptised?

The next chapter will help you think through the practical details. But before you get there, take a moment with your church leader to answer that question honestly. Because everything in Chapter 4 assumes the answer is yes.

elements.term1: A decision to make
elements.question1: What should you do before the next chapter?
elements.answer1: Talk with your church leader about whether you are ready to be baptised. If the answer is yes — even a tentative yes — come to Chapter 4 ready to start thinking practically. If you are still unsure, bring your specific questions and hesitations to that conversation. Chapter 5 includes a full section on common questions people ask, which may address what is holding you back.

elements.term1: This chapter is practical
elements.question1: The first three chapters helped you understand what baptism is, who it is for, and what community it connects you to. This chapter helps you prepare for the day itself. It assumes you have decided — or are seriously leaning toward — being baptised. Even if you are not quite there yet, read through it so you know what to expect.

elements.text1:
Adam had said yes.

He had said it quietly, to his pastor, after the third session of their baptism preparation. He had not made an announcement. He had not felt a dramatic surge of emotion. He had simply looked at everything he had read and discussed, weighed it honestly, and said: Yes. I want to do this. I think I should do this. Let’s work out how.

His pastor had smiled and opened a notebook. “Good,” he said. “Now the fun part.”

Adam had not expected baptism preparation to involve questions like what are you going to wear? and who do you want to invite? But there was something grounding about it — something that made the whole thing feel real in a new way. This was not abstract theology any more. This was happening. On a specific day, in a specific place, with specific people watching.

That is what this chapter is for.
Part 1 — Making the Decision

Before the practical details, it is worth pausing to mark the decision itself.

Baptism is a command of Jesus. It is not a suggestion for particularly devoted Christians, or an optional extra for those who enjoy ceremonies. In the New Testament, the pattern is clear: believe, repent, be baptised. The people in Acts 2 did not deliberate for months. The Ethiopian official in Acts 8 did not wait for a convenient season. They responded to the gospel with urgency and joy.

That does not mean you should be pressured into baptism before you understand it — this study exists precisely so that you can understand it well. But it does mean that once you understand it, delay for its own sake is not wisdom. It is disobedience dressed up as caution.

If you have worked through the first three chapters, discussed them with your church leader, and are confident that you have personally put your faith in Jesus Christ — then the question is not really whether to be baptised. It is when.

elements.question1: Have you made the decision to be baptised? If yes — what brought you to that point? If not yet — what is the specific thing that is still holding you back?

deeper.question1: Is there a difference between being ready to be baptised and understanding baptism fully? Do you think you need to understand everything before you obey? What does the example of the New Testament Christians suggest?

elements.term1: “I don’t feel good enough to be baptised.”
elements.question1: What if you still feel unworthy — like you need to reach a higher level of Christian maturity before you take this step?
elements.answer1:
You are not good enough. Neither is anyone else who has ever been baptised. That is precisely the point.

Baptism is not a graduation ceremony for mature Christians. It is the first public step of a new one. It is for repentant people — people who know they are not good enough, who have stopped pretending otherwise, and who have thrown themselves on the mercy of Jesus. The people baptised in Acts 2 had believed the gospel that same day. They had not yet had time to prove anything.

If you wait until you feel worthy, you will wait forever. The gospel does not say “clean yourself up and then come.” It says “come as you are — and be washed.”

elements.term1: “What if I am baptised and then mess it up afterwards?”
elements.question1: Is it better to wait until you are more spiritually stable, so that you do not let people down after your baptism?
elements.answer1:
You will mess it up afterwards. That is not a reason to delay — it is simply the reality of the Christian life. Baptism does not make you immune to sin. It does not guarantee a perfect track record. What it does is mark a decisive turning point — a public declaration that you have changed direction, that sin is no longer your master, and that you are committed to going God’s way with God’s help.

When you do sin after baptism — and you will — the answer is not to wish you had waited longer before being baptised. The answer is to do what Christians have always done: confess it, repent, receive forgiveness, and keep going. Baptism is the beginning of a life of following Jesus, not the end of a probation period.

Do not let the fear of future failure become an excuse to delay present obedience.

elements.text1: Part 2 — When and Where

Once the decision is made, the most important practical step is to fix a date. This sounds simple — but it matters more than it seems. Without a specific date, baptism becomes an intention rather than a commitment, and intentions have a way of remaining perpetually in the future. Fix the date. Put it in your calendar. Tell your church leader. Tell your family.

elements.question1: Have you spoken to your church leader about a possible date for your baptism? Is there a date already in mind — either one the church has planned, or one that works for you and your family?

elements.text1:
When you are baptised matters for one important reason: you want as many of the people you love to be there as possible. Baptism is a witness — a public declaration of what Jesus has done for you and what he could do for them too. The people you invite are not just an audience. They are hearing the gospel in one of its most vivid forms. Choose a time that gives them the best chance of being there. Do not worry about whether they will come. Invite them anyway.

Where you are baptised will usually be decided by your church. Most churches have a preferred location — a baptistry, a swimming pool, a river, or another body of water — and they are set up so that people can see and hear clearly. If you have questions about the location, ask your church leader.

elements.question1: Who are the people — family, friends, neighbours, colleagues — you most want to invite to your baptism? Write down their names. Is there anyone on that list who does not yet know Jesus? How might seeing your baptism be an opportunity for them?

deeper.question1: Is there anyone you are nervous about inviting — someone whose reaction you are uncertain of, or who might not respond well? What is holding you back from inviting them, and what might it mean for them to be there?

elements.text1: Part 3 — Who Will Be There

Who will baptise you?

Your church leader will tell you who will baptise you — it will normally be one of the church’s leaders or pastors. It is also a good idea to have a trusted Christian friend or family member present to help with practical things: holding your towel, looking after your belongings, and being a familiar, steady face nearby.

Who will you invite?

Think widely. Baptism is not just a church event — it is an evangelistic opportunity. The people in your life who do not yet follow Jesus will hear, perhaps for the first time in a direct and personal way, what Jesus has done for you. You do not need to prepare a theological argument. You just need to let them see what has happened to you.

elements.term1: “My parents don’t want me to be baptised.”
elements.question1: What should you do if the people closest to you are opposed to your baptism?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most painful situations a new Christian can face, and it requires both wisdom and courage.

Start by making sure they understand what you are doing and why. Many people’s resistance to baptism comes from misunderstanding — they may think it means you are rejecting your family, joining a strange group, or being reckless. Explain clearly what baptism means: that it is a declaration of your personal faith in Jesus Christ, and an act of obedience to him.

Ask your church leader to meet with them if they are willing. Prayer is essential — not as a last resort, but throughout.

If you are still young and living under your parents’ authority, it may be wise to wait — not forever, but for a season — while continuing to pray and explain. Honour and obedience to parents is also a command of God. Hold both commands carefully, with your church leader’s guidance.

If you are an adult, and you have explained your reasons clearly and your family still objects, then — gently, lovingly, without making it a confrontation — you can go ahead. Your ultimate obedience is to Jesus. But do everything you can to help your family understand before you do.

elements.text1: Part 4 — What Will Happen on the Day

What will you wear?

Choose clothes that are practical for going fully underwater. Avoid anything too loose, too light, or likely to become see-through when wet. Dark colours are usually a safe choice. Bring a towel and a full change of clothes, and make sure you take off your watch and remove your phone from your pocket before you go in.

What will you sing?

Many baptism services include worship. Your church may invite you to suggest a song or two — perhaps something that has been particularly meaningful to you in your journey to faith. Ask your church leader whether this is something you can contribute to.

elements.question1: Is there a song, a hymn, or a piece of music that has been especially significant in your journey to faith — something that captures what Jesus has done for you? What is it, and why does it matter to you?

elements.text1: Part 5 — What You Will Say

This is the part that matters most — and the part that many people feel most nervous about. But it is also the part with the most potential to affect the people watching.

Many churches will ask you to answer a set of questions as part of the baptism service — a public declaration of what you believe about Jesus and what he has done for you. Your church leader will tell you what those questions are in advance. There are no trick questions. They are simply asking you to say out loud what you already believe.

Many churches also invite the person being baptised to share their testimony — the story of how they came to faith. This is not a performance. It is not a speech. It is simply your story, told honestly. And it is one of the most powerful things a person watching can hear.

elements.term1: Three parts to your story
elements.question1: How do you tell your story in a way that is honest, clear, and points to Jesus rather than to yourself?
elements.answer1:
A testimony has three natural parts:

Before — What was your life like before you came to faith in Jesus? You do not need to make this dramatic or list every sin you have ever committed. But be honest about who you were, what you were living for, and what was missing.

How — How did you come to faith? What did you hear, or read, or experience? Was there a moment of decision, or a gradual realisation? Who was involved? What did God use to reach you?

Since — What difference has Jesus made? How have you changed? What is different about the way you live, think, or see the world? What are you still learning?

Remember: your testimony is not really about you. It is about what Jesus has done. Keep him at the centre. And do not believe the lie that your story is boring — no story of a person being brought from darkness to light, from death to life, is boring.

elements.question1: Using the three-part shape above, write a draft of your testimony here. Keep it honest and keep Jesus at the centre. Aim for something you could say in two to three minutes.

deeper.question1: Read through what you have written. Is Jesus clearly at the centre — or does it sound more like a story about your own journey and growth? What would you change to make his work more visible in the telling?

elements.question1: Some people feel nervous about speaking in front of others. If that is you, what would help you feel more prepared? Have you considered writing it out in full so you can read from it if you need to?

elements.term1: “What if people mock me or treat me badly because I’ve been baptised?”
elements.question1: Is fear of others’ reactions a good reason to delay or avoid baptism?
elements.answer1:
It is an understandable reason. It is not a good one.

Jesus was clear with his disciples that following him would not always be comfortable. People who hated him would, at times, direct that hostility toward those who followed him. He did not promise that baptism would make life easier — he promised that he would be with his people always, to the very end of the age.

If you live openly for Jesus — telling people about him, baptised in his name — some negative reaction may come. It might come from family. It might come from friends. It might come from your community. When it does, it is not a sign that something has gone wrong. It is a sign that your faith is visible and your baptism has been noticed.

Jesus’ promise is not that it will be easy. It is that he will see you through. Hold on to that.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects where you are right now in your baptism preparation.
statements1: I have made — or am ready to make — the decision to be baptised.
I have discussed a date for my baptism with my church leader.
I have a clear sense of who I want to invite to my baptism.
I can tell the story of how I came to faith in my own words, with Jesus at the centre.
I feel ready for what will happen on the day — practically and spiritually.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Adam said yes quietly, without drama — after weighing things honestly and deciding it was simply the right thing to do. Is that where you are? What does your “yes” look like, and what has brought you to it?

When you picture the moment of coming up out of the water — the faces watching, the declaration made, the belonging confirmed — how do you feel? What are you looking forward to? What are you still nervous about?
Baptism is a beginning, not an ending. What do you hope the life that follows your baptism will look like — in your church, your relationships, your walk with God?

elements.text1:
There is a moment in every baptism — just before the person goes under the water — when everything goes quiet. The congregation stills. The person being baptised takes a breath. And for just a second, the weight of what is about to happen is felt by everyone in the room.

Then they go under. And they come back up. And everything that the gospel declares about them — washed, buried, raised, belonging — is acted out in front of the watching world.

It does not take long. But it changes something. Not in the water. In the person. And in the community that receives them.

May the Lord bless you in your baptism. And may he get all the glory.

elements.term1: My Baptism Notes and Common Questions
elements.question1: What comes next in this study?
elements.answer1: Chapter 5 has two parts. The first is a set of planning notes — spaces to record the practical details of your baptism as you finalise them with your church leader. The second is a collection of common questions people ask about baptism, with honest answers for each one. If there is a question this study has raised but not yet answered, you may find it there.

chapters.chapterTitle1: My Baptism Notes & Some Questions People Ask

elements.term1: Two parts — planning and questions
elements.question1: This final chapter has two purposes. The first is practical: it gives you space to record the details of your baptism as you finalise them with your church leader. The second is theological: it collects the most common questions people ask about baptism and gives honest answers to each one. Work through Part 1 with your church leader. Use Part 2 whenever a question arises — before, during, or after your preparation.

elements.text1: Part 1 — My Baptism Notes

Use this section to record the practical details of your baptism as they are confirmed. Some of these you will decide yourself. Others will be decided with your church leader. Do not leave this section blank — writing things down makes them real, and gives you something to look back on long after the day itself.
The Basics

elements.question1: When?

deeper.question1: What is the date and time of your baptism?

elements.question1: Where?

deeper.question1: Where will your baptism take place?

elements.question1: Who will baptise me?

deeper.question1: Which church leader or pastor will baptise you?

elements.question1: Who will help me on the day?

deeper.question1: Is there a trusted friend or family member who will be there to help with practical things — holding your towel, looking after your belongings, being a steady presence?

elements.text1: People and Preparation

elements.question1: Who will I invite?

deeper.question1: List the people you plan to invite — family, friends, neighbours, colleagues. Include people who do not yet follow Jesus.

elements.question1: What will I wear?

deeper.question1: What have you chosen to wear? Remember: practical, not see-through when wet, dark colours are usually best. Bring a towel and a full change of clothes.

elements.question1: What will we sing?

deeper.question1: Are there songs or hymns you would like included in the service — pieces that have been meaningful in your journey to faith? Note them here to discuss with your church leader.

elements.text1: My Testimony

Your testimony is the most personal thing you will do on the day. Use the space below to write it out in full — or at least in enough detail that you can speak from it confidently. Remember the three-part shape: before, how, and since. And remember: keep Jesus at the centre. It is his story as much as yours.

elements.question1: What I was like before I was saved…

deeper.question1: Describe your life before you came to faith in Jesus. What were you living for? What was missing? Be honest, but keep the focus on what was spiritually true — not a catalogue of every mistake.

elements.question1: How Jesus saved me…

deeper.question1: How did you come to faith? What did you hear, read, or experience? Was there a moment of decision, or a gradual turning? Who was involved? What did God use to reach you?

elements.question1: What difference Jesus is making in my life…

deeper.question1: How has your life changed since you came to faith? What is different about the way you think, live, or see the world? What are you still learning?

elements.question1: What I would be like without Jesus…

deeper.question1: Looking back — and looking honestly at who you are — what would your life look like if Jesus had not found you? This is a powerful thing to say aloud on the day.

elements.term1: Write it down and show someone
elements.question1: Should you memorise your testimony, or is it better to read from notes?
elements.answer1:
Both are fine — but writing it out fully is always a good idea. If you get nervous on the day and lose your train of thought, having something written to refer to is far better than silence. It also means you can show it to your church leader beforehand, so they can help you make sure it is clear, that Jesus is at the centre, and that nothing important has been left out.

Some people are naturally comfortable speaking in front of others. Many are not. If you are nervous, that is completely normal — and it does not mean you should not do it. It means you should prepare well. Practise saying it aloud at home, ideally in front of a trusted friend or family member, before the day.

elements.text1: Part 2 — Some Questions People Ask

The questions below are some of the most common ones people bring to baptism preparation. You may have already encountered some of them in earlier chapters — here they are collected together so that you can find them easily, return to them, and share them with others who have questions of their own.

If your question is not here, your church leader is the best person to ask.

elements.term1: When does the Holy Spirit enter my life?
elements.question1: Some people believe you receive the Holy Spirit at some point after conversion — as a separate “baptism in the Spirit.” Is that right?
elements.answer1:
No. When you come to faith in Jesus Christ, the Holy Spirit comes to live in you at that same moment — not later, not after a second experience, but immediately and fully.

John the Baptist spoke of Jesus baptising with the Holy Spirit, and this led some to think that Spirit-baptism happens after water baptism, as a second step. But John was speaking in terms of history — at the point he said those words, Jesus had not yet sent the Spirit. Once Jesus sent the Holy Spirit to his disciples at Pentecost (Acts 2), John’s prophecy was fulfilled. From that point on, every person who believes in Jesus receives the Holy Spirit the moment they believe.

Indeed, it is the Spirit who enables you to believe in the first place. If you have put your faith in Jesus Christ, the Holy Spirit is already at work in you. You do not need to wait for a second experience. You already have him — and he already has you.

elements.term1: I was baptised as a baby — won’t that do?
elements.question1: If you were baptised as an infant, do you need to be baptised again as a believer?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most genuinely contested questions in Christian theology, and good Christians disagree about it. It is worth understanding the disagreement honestly.

Some traditions — including many Anglican, Catholic, Lutheran, and Presbyterian churches — practice infant baptism. They believe it is a sign of the covenant, parallel to circumcision in the Old Testament, and that it marks a child’s belonging to the covenant community, anticipating the personal faith that will come later. These traditions do not believe infant baptism brings about salvation, but they do believe it is a valid and meaningful act.

Other traditions — including most Baptist and many independent evangelical churches — believe that baptism in the New Testament is always given to people who are personally professing faith. On this view, infant baptism, however well-intentioned, is not what the New Testament has in mind — and a person who comes to personal faith should be baptised as a believer, even if they were baptised as an infant.

The view of this study is the second one. Baptism is a sign of new spiritual birth — of a personal turning to Christ — and it belongs to those who can give a credible, personal profession of faith.

If you were baptised as an infant and have now come to personal faith, talk this through carefully and prayerfully with your church leader. It is an important conversation, and the answer may depend on your specific situation and your church’s convictions.

elements.term1: Why do I need to go all the way under? Can’t I just be sprinkled?
elements.question1: Does baptism have to involve full immersion — going completely under the water — or is sprinkling an acceptable alternative?
elements.answer1:
The Greek word translated “baptise” in the New Testament means to wash, dip, plunge, or submerge. The weight of the word itself points toward immersion rather than sprinkling.

More importantly, the meaning of baptism — as we explored in Chapter 2 — is inseparable from the pictures of burial and resurrection. When you go completely under the water and rise back up, you are enacting the death of the old life and the birth of the new. Full immersion makes that picture vivid and unmistakeable. Sprinkling conveys the picture of washing but loses the picture of burial and resurrection.

For these reasons, full immersion is strongly preferred — it is the method most consistent with the meaning baptism carries in the New Testament.

That said, there are genuine exceptions. If full immersion is not physically possible due to disability or serious health concerns, a church leader can help discern what is appropriate. The form of baptism matters — but the person’s faith and the meaning of the act matter most.

elements.term1: Why was Jesus baptised if he had no sin?
elements.question1: Jesus had never sinned — so why did he need to be baptised? What was he doing?
elements.answer1:
John the Baptist raised exactly this question when Jesus came to him at the Jordan River. John tried to stop him, saying it should be the other way around — Jesus should be baptising John. But Jesus told him to go ahead, “to fulfil all righteousness” (Matthew 3:15).

There are at least three things happening in Jesus’ baptism. First, he was simply doing what was right — and God the Father confirmed it immediately, declaring from heaven that he was well pleased with his Son. Second, he was giving a sign of what he was going to do: his own death, burial, and resurrection — the very realities that every believer’s baptism points back to. Third, he was identifying himself with sinners. He stood in the place of the people he came to save — not because he shared their guilt, but because he was going to bear it for them.

Jesus’ baptism was unique. He did not need what baptism declares — forgiveness, new birth, freedom from sin. But by being baptised, he endorsed the practice and connected it to the heart of his saving work.

elements.term1: I don’t feel good enough to be baptised.
elements.question1: What if you feel that you need to reach a higher level of holiness or maturity before you are ready to be baptised?
elements.answer1:
You are not good enough. Neither is anyone who has ever been baptised — including every apostle, every martyr, and every Christian in the history of the church. That is precisely the point of the gospel.

Baptism is not for people who have earned it. It is not a reward for those who have demonstrated sufficient spiritual progress. It is for repentant people — people who know they fall short, who have stopped pretending otherwise, and who have cast themselves on the mercy of Jesus.

The people baptised in Acts 2 were baptised the same day they believed. They had not had time to clean up their lives or prove their sincerity. What they had done was accept the gospel, repent, and obey.

If you are waiting until you feel good enough, you misunderstand what baptism is for. It does not declare that you have arrived. It declares that you have turned — that you have left one direction and are now facing another, with Jesus ahead of you rather than behind you.

elements.term1: What if I am baptised and then mess it up afterwards?
elements.question1: Is it better to wait until you are more spiritually stable, in case you fall into sin after your baptism and let people down?
elements.answer1:
You will sin after your baptism. So will everyone else who has ever been baptised. The question is not whether you will mess it up — you will — but what you do when you do.

Baptism does not make you perfect. It does not immunise you against sin or guarantee that you will never struggle. What it does is mark a decisive, public turning point — a declaration that sin is no longer your master, that you have changed direction, and that you are committed to going God’s way with God’s help.

When you fall — and the New Testament assumes you will — the answer is confession, repentance, and returning to God. He has not stopped being your Father. His forgiveness has not expired. 1 John 1:9 is written for baptised Christians who have sinned: “If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and purify us from all unrighteousness.”

Do not let fear of future failure become an excuse for present disobedience. Be baptised. Keep going. Come back when you stray. That is the Christian life.

elements.term1: What if people mock or mistreat me for being baptised?
elements.question1: Is fear of others’ reactions — from family, friends, or community — a valid reason to delay or avoid baptism?
elements.answer1:
It is an understandable fear. In some contexts, being baptised carries real social cost — it can affect family relationships, friendships, or community standing. That is not a small thing, and it should not be dismissed lightly.

But it is not a good reason to disobey Jesus.

Jesus was clear with his disciples that following him in a visible, public way would sometimes generate hostility. He did not promise that baptism would make life comfortable. He promised that he would be with his people — always, to the very end of the age (Matthew 28:20). And he promised that those who lose things for his sake will find them — that the cost of following him is always worth it in the end.

If you face real danger as a result of being baptised, speak honestly with your church leader about how to proceed wisely. There is a difference between social awkwardness and genuine risk, and wisdom applies differently in each case. But in general: do not let the fear of what others will think or say keep you from obeying the one who has all authority in heaven and on earth.

elements.term1: Do I need to be baptised to take part in communion?
elements.question1: What is the relationship between baptism and communion — and does being unbaptised mean you cannot take communion?
